title: Peanut brittle
subtitle: Crisp caramel slab with salted peanuts
excerpt: Caster sugar and golden syrup cooked to hard-crack stage, then poured over salted peanuts and left to set into a shatteringly crisp sheet. Needs a sugar thermometer and close attention at the stove.
sourceNotes: Brittle confections made from boiled sugar and nuts appear in Victorian confectionery manuals. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, lists boiled sugar confections under the confectionery chapter. The hard-crack stage (149-154°C) is the foundational temperature range for all crisp-set sugar confections.

recipe.batchNotes: Doubles well. Use a larger heavy-based pan; the mixture foams when the butter goes in.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps in a dry airtight tin for 2 weeks. Humidity softens brittle quickly.

glossaryTerms.definition: The confectionery temperature stage reached when sugar syrup hits 149-154°C. A drop dropped into cold water forms brittle threads that snap cleanly. Sugar at hard-crack sets shatteringly crisp at room temperature.

content.text: Peanut brittle is a caramel taken to

and poured thin over salted peanuts. The result is a pale amber slab that shatters into irregular pieces. A sugar thermometer is not optional: the window between pale caramel and burnt sugar is a matter of seconds.

Boiling sugar reaches 150°C and sticks to skin. Keep a bowl of cold water near the stove. If hot sugar contacts skin, run cold water over it for at least ten minutes and seek medical advice if the burn is large.

Line a baking tray with baking paper and set it near the hob. Put the {{caster-sugar}}, {{golden-syrup}}, and 3 tbsp of water in a medium heavy-based saucepan. Stir over a low heat until the sugar dissolves, then stop stirring. Increase the heat to medium-high and bring to a boil. Cook without stirring until the thermometer reads 149-154°C (hard-crack). The syrup will be pale gold. If it starts to colour unevenly, swirl the pan gently.

Remove the pan from the heat. Add the {{unsalted-butter}} and stir quickly until it melts, then tip in the {{salted-peanuts}} and stir to coat. Pour immediately onto the lined tray and spread thin with the back of a spoon. Work fast: the sugar firms within seconds. Leave to cool completely at room temperature, at least 30 minutes, then snap into pieces by hand.
